what_it_does: An acceptance test script that builds a throwaway git repository, seeds six registry directories, and exercises Suite.create_* for four pure-data cognition registries (mark_type, generation_policy, relation_type, ai_tic), verifies malformed specs and duplicate ids are refused, checks that available_inputs advertises all six registries including lifter/form, and tests the marks API (mark, marks_for, marks_by_type) with fail-loud validation of registered mark types.
observations: Uses a temporary git repo to avoid touching real registry directories or history.
Seeds six registry directories (lifters, mark_types, generation_policies, relation_types, ai_tics, forms) with baseline modules.
Tests create_* only for the four data registries; explicitly asserts lifter/form have no create_* because they carry callables.
Each successful create_* writes a <dir>/<id>.py file, makes a git commit, and the entry is immediately discoverable.
Malformed specs are caught by the registry's own gate and must not write files or commit.
Duplicate ids are refused via ValueError.
available_inputs is expected to list entries from all six registries.
Marks are tested by target retrieval (marks_for) and by type retrieval (marks_by_type).
Unknown mark_types raise ValueError at the Suite gate and are not persisted.
Final FLOOR check inspects git log messages to confirm data commits and no [self-build] dispatch.
standouts: Tightly couples P2 registry-authoring acceptance with M1 marks API acceptance in one wiring test.
Explicitly encodes the architectural rule that data registries are declarative-direct while lifter/form are code-authoring and gated.
Uses real git commits as the revertible safety net for every data write.
conventions: Inline acceptance-test style with a global PASS counter and a simple check() helper rather than a pytest harness.
Requirement labels (P2.1, P2.2, M1.1, FLOOR) map directly to code sections and the module docstring.
Centralized spec dictionaries (GOOD, BAD, SEEDS, DIRS, ALL_REG_ATTRS) drive parameterized-style loops.
Subprocess wrapper for git commands to inspect repository state.
concerns: P2.2 catches bare Exception, which could mask bugs unrelated to gate refusal.
Global mutable state (PASS, ok) makes the script non-thread-safe and harder to reuse.
Files are written with open(...).write(...) without context managers.
The test relies on hasattr(suite, 'create_lifter') and hasattr(suite, 'create_form'), which may be brittle if methods exist but raise.
shutil.rmtree(work, ignore_errors=True) can hide cleanup failures.
No isolation of stdout/stderr from subprocess calls beyond capture_output.
notes: A wiring acceptance test that proves pure-data cognition registries can be created, committed, and discovered live while marks are gated by registered types.
questions: What is the exact gate implementation that refuses malformed specs in each registry?
What does the operator approval / surfaced-item flow look like for lifter/form code-authoring?
What is the [self-build] dispatch mechanism referenced in the FLOOR check?
How does the Suite discover and reload registry modules after a file write?
What is the intended schema for mark evidence, source_pass, and value typing?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose (P2 create_* wiring plus M1 marks API) matches the actual test coverage.
apparent_themes: Git-backed safety net for data writes
Separation of data registries from callable code registries
intention_signals: The file is trying to demonstrate by execution that the system can author pure-data cognition registry entries directly, commit them safely, and enforce that marks can only use registered types, while keeping callable-bearing registries out of the data-authoring path.
novelty: It treats registry authoring as a first-class data write with live discovery and git commit verification, and it explicitly encodes the distinction between data-create and code-authoring registries as a testable invariant.
contribution: A runnable integration test that documents and validates the expected behavior of Suite.create_* for four data registries, the refusal behavior for malformed and duplicate entries, the advertisement of all six registries in available_inputs, and the marks API round-trip with fail-loud type validation.
summary_for_embedding: An acceptance test for declarative-direct authoring of four pure-data cognition registries and fail-loud marks API round-tripping in a throwaway git repo.
intention_for_embedding: To prove by use that pure-data registry entries can be created, committed, and discovered live, while marks are gated by registered mark types and callable registries remain code-authoring only.

symbols.does: Inline assertion helper that tallies passing checks and flips a global failure flag.
symbols.description: A minimal test assertion function used throughout the script. It increments the global PASS counter and prints an ok line when cond is true; otherwise it sets the global ok flag to False and prints a FAIL line.

symbols.does: Runs a git command in a given directory and returns the stripped stdout, raising on failure.
symbols.description: A convenience wrapper around subprocess.run that executes git with -C root and the supplied arguments, captures output, checks the return code, and returns the stripped standard output; used to inspect the throwaway repository's commits and state.
